name: Frederick Burton
description: Black liberation prisoner who has maintained his innocence for over four decades. Convicted in 1971 of conspiracy in the August 1970 shooting death of Philadelphia police sergeant Frank Von Colln at the Cobbs Creek Park guardhouse, allegedly carried out by a group called 'the Revolutionaries.' Prior to his arrest, Burton worked at a phone company, was a respected member of his community, and his wife was pregnant with twins. He has been incarcerated in Pennsylvania prisons since 1970 — among the longest-held Black political prisoners in the United States.

cases.charges: Conspiracy / murder of Philadelphia police sergeant Frank Von Colln

name: Ronald Reed
description: Former Black United Front activist convicted twenty-five years after the fact for the May 22, 1970 sniper shooting of St. Paul, Minnesota police officer James Sackett. Reed came of age organizing at St. Paul Central High School, demanding Black history courses and confronting racist teachers, and helped integrate Minnesota college campuses. After being drawn toward revolutionary politics, he engaged in political street theater with other young Black revolutionaries in St. Paul. Arrested in 2005 and convicted in 2006 of first-degree murder and conspiracy to commit first-degree murder; sentenced to life in prison. The conviction relied heavily on jailhouse informants.

cases.institution_name: Minnesota Correctional Facility — Oak Park Heights
cases.charges: First-degree murder and conspiracy to commit first-degree murder (1970 killing of St. Paul officer James Sackett)

name: Casey Brezik
description: Anarchist convicted of a September 2010 knife attack at Metropolitan Community College in Independence, Missouri during which he allegedly attempted to assassinate Missouri Governor Jay Nixon, who was scheduled to speak at the campus that day. Brezik stabbed a college dean instead in the confusion. Convicted June 2013 of three counts of assault and two counts of armed criminal action, sentenced to twelve years on each count plus seven years on a second assault count, all running concurrently.

cases.charges: Assault, armed criminal action (attempted attack on Governor Jay Nixon)

name: Eric King
description: Anarchist and vegan political prisoner arrested in 2014 for allegedly throwing two Molotov cocktails at the Kansas City, Missouri office of U.S. Congressman Emanuel Cleaver in protest of police killings during the Ferguson uprising. Charged federally with using a dangerous instrument to oppose, impede, intimidate, and interfere with a federal official. Pleaded guilty in 2016 and was sentenced to ten years in federal prison. While incarcerated King faced repeated retaliation by Bureau of Prisons staff, including being placed in long-term solitary confinement and additional charges following a 2018 assault he maintained was in self-defense (he was acquitted of those charges in March 2022).

cases.charges: Using a dangerous instrument to interfere with a federal official (Molotov cocktails at congressman's office)

name: Connor Stevens
description: Cleveland 4 defendant. One of five young men entrapped by an FBI informant, Shaquille Azir, in an alleged 2012 plot to blow up the Northfield Center Township bridge over the Cuyahoga River south of Cleveland, Ohio during May Day protests against the Group of Eight (G8) and NATO. The informant provided the plot, transportation, financing, and the inert 'explosives' supplied by undercover FBI agents. Pleaded guilty in 2012 and was sentenced to roughly 8 years in federal prison.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and attempted use of weapons of mass destruction (FBI-entrapment Cuyahoga bridge plot)

name: Douglas L. Wright
description: Cleveland 4 defendant, the oldest of the five young Occupy Cleveland activists entrapped by FBI informant Shaquille Azir in an alleged 2012 plot to bomb the Northfield Center Township bridge over the Cuyahoga River. The plot, target, financing, and dummy 'explosives' were all furnished by the informant and undercover FBI agents. Pleaded guilty in 2012 to conspiracy and attempted use of weapons of mass destruction. Sentenced in November 2012 to 11 years and 8 months in federal prison — the longest sentence of the group.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and attempted use of weapons of mass destruction (FBI-entrapment Cuyahoga bridge plot)

name: Brent Betterly
description: One of the NATO 3, arrested on May 16, 2012 in the lead-up to anti-NATO summit protests in Chicago. Originally charged with 11 felony counts under Illinois state material-aid-to-terrorism statutes after two Chicago Police Department undercover officers known as 'Mo' and 'Gloves' attempted to entrap him and two co-defendants. Acquitted of all terrorism counts on February 7, 2014. Found guilty of possession of an incendiary device with intent to commit arson, and of mob action. Sentenced April 25, 2014 to six years in Illinois state prison.

cases.charges: Possession of an incendiary device with intent to commit arson; mob action

name: Jared Chase
description: One of the NATO 3, arrested on May 16, 2012 ahead of anti-NATO summit protests in Chicago. Originally charged with 11 felony terrorism counts under Illinois state law after entrapment by Chicago Police Department undercover officers 'Mo' and 'Gloves.' Acquitted of all terrorism counts on February 7, 2014; found guilty of possession of an incendiary device with intent to commit arson, and of mob action. Sentenced April 25, 2014 to eight years in Illinois state prison — the longest of the three. Chase, who has Huntington's disease, faced additional charges while incarcerated.

cases.charges: Possession of an incendiary device with intent to commit arson; mob action

name: Brian Church
description: One of the NATO 3, arrested May 16, 2012 in advance of anti-NATO summit protests in Chicago. Originally charged with 11 felony terrorism counts under Illinois state material-aid-to-terrorism statutes — the first prosecution under that statute — after two Chicago Police Department undercover officers, 'Mo' and 'Gloves,' attempted to entrap him and two co-defendants. Acquitted of all terrorism counts on February 7, 2014; found guilty of possession of an incendiary device with intent to commit arson, and of mob action. Sentenced April 25, 2014 to five years in Illinois state prison.

cases.charges: Possession of an incendiary device with intent to commit arson; mob action

name: Brian Vaillancourt
description: Anarchist arrested in February 2013 in Chicago and charged with attempting to set fire to a McDonald's restaurant. Pleaded guilty in 2013 and was sentenced to nine years in Illinois state prison.

cases.charges: Attempted arson (McDonald's restaurant)

name: Byron Shane Chubbuck
description: Cherokee and Choctaw indigenous-rights activist serving 80 years in federal prison for a string of bank robberies in 1998-1999. A confidential informant told the FBI that Chubbuck — who used the nom de guerre 'Oso Blanco' (White Bear) — was robbing banks to send funds to the Zapatista National Liberation Army (EZLN) rebellion in Chiapas, Mexico. Convicted of bank robbery, aggravated assault on an FBI agent, escape, and firearms charges. Held in long-term federal custody including USP Lewisburg.

cases.charges: Bank robbery, aggravated assault on FBI agent, escape, firearms charges

name: Barrett Brown
description: Journalist, essayist, and founder of Project PM, an online research collective investigating the private intelligence contracting industry. Indicted in October 2012 on three counts after the FBI raided his apartment over alleged Anonymous-related activity; indicted again in December 2012 on twelve counts after he pasted a hyperlink to a publicly available dump of emails from intelligence firm Strategic Forecasting (Stratfor), hacked by Anonymous/LulzSec. Indicted a third time in January 2013 over the FBI raids. In April 2014 he pleaded guilty to three reduced counts (accessory after the fact, interference with the execution of a search warrant, and transmitting a threat). Sentenced January 2015 to 63 months in federal prison and ordered to pay $890,000 in restitution.

cases.charges: Accessory after the fact; interference with execution of a search warrant; transmitting a threat (Stratfor hack hyperlink case)

name: Jeremy Hammond
description: Anarchist computer hacker from Chicago and member of Anonymous/LulzSec affiliate AntiSec. Convicted in November 2013 of leaking the personal information of approximately 860,000 customers of the private intelligence firm Strategic Forecasting (Stratfor) via the whistleblowing site WikiLeaks; the leak revealed that Stratfor was spying on activists, journalists, and dissidents on behalf of corporate and U.S. government clients. Sentenced to ten years in federal prison plus three years of supervised release — the maximum under his plea agreement. Released October 2019; jailed again in 2019 for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating WikiLeaks; released November 2020.

cases.charges: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act violations (Stratfor hack)

name: Walter Bond
description: Animal Liberation Front activist who claimed responsibility under the moniker 'ALF Lone Wolf' for three 2010 arsons: the Sheepskin Factory in Denver, Colorado (April 30, 2010); the Tandy Industrial Leather Factory in Salt Lake City, Utah (June 5, 2010); and the Tiburon Fine Dining restaurant in Sandy, Utah, which served foie gras (July 3, 2010). Arrested July 22, 2010 and pleaded guilty to all three arsons plus one count under the Animal Enterprise Terrorism Act. Sentenced to a total of 12 years 3 months in federal prison; released in 2021.

cases.charges: Three counts of arson; Animal Enterprise Terrorism Act

name: Andrew Mickel
description: Anti-police militant who shot and killed Red Bluff, California police officer David Mobilio at a 24-hour gas station on November 19, 2002. The crime might have gone unsolved had Mickel not posted on the website Indymedia six days later: 'Hello Everyone, my name's Andy. I killed a Police Officer in Red Bluff, California in a motion to bring attention to, and halt, the police-state tactics that have come to be used throughout our country.' Mickel, a former Army Ranger, represented himself at trial and declared himself a 'prisoner of war.' Convicted of first-degree murder in April 2005 and sentenced to death; held on California's Death Row at San Quentin until California abolished its death row and converted his sentence to life without parole.

cases.charges: First-degree murder (killing of Red Bluff police officer David Mobilio)
cases.sentence: Death (later commuted to life without parole)

name: Megan Rice
description: Roman Catholic Sister of the Holy Child Jesus and lifelong anti-nuclear-weapons activist. At age 82, Rice and fellow Plowshares activists Michael R. Walli and Greg Boertje-Obed broke into the Y-12 National Security Complex in Oak Ridge, Tennessee on July 28, 2012 — the most security-sensitive uranium processing facility in the U.S. — hung banners, splashed human blood, and chalked anti-nuclear messages on the wall of the Highly Enriched Uranium Materials Facility. The breach prompted a year-long shutdown and Congressional hearings on Y-12 security. Convicted in May 2013 of sabotaging the national defense and damaging federal property. Sentenced February 2014 to nearly three years in federal prison. Convictions vacated on appeal in May 2015; released the same month. Died October 10, 2021.
affiliation: Society of the Holy Child Jesus

cases.charges: Sabotage of the national defense; damaging federal property (Y-12 Transform Now Plowshares action)

name: Michael R. Walli
description: Catholic Worker and Vietnam veteran turned anti-war and anti-nuclear activist. With Sister Megan Rice and Greg Boertje-Obed, breached the Y-12 National Security Complex in Oak Ridge, Tennessee on July 28, 2012, the United States' principal storehouse of weapons-grade uranium, hanging banners and splashing blood on the Highly Enriched Uranium Materials Facility. Convicted in May 2013 of sabotaging the national defense and damaging federal property. Sentenced February 2014 to just over five years in federal prison. The sabotage conviction was vacated on appeal in May 2015 and Walli was resentenced to time served.

cases.charges: Sabotage of the national defense; damaging federal property (Y-12 Transform Now Plowshares action)
cases.convicted: Yes — May 2013 (sabotage conviction vacated on appeal May 2015)

name: Gregory Boertje-Obed
description: Catholic Worker, peace activist and U.S. Army veteran. With Sister Megan Rice and Michael R. Walli, breached the Y-12 National Security Complex in Oak Ridge, Tennessee on July 28, 2012 — cutting fences and crossing into the heart of the Highly Enriched Uranium Materials Facility, hanging banners reading 'Swords into Plowshares: Spears into Pruning Hooks' and splashing baby bottles of human blood on the walls. Convicted May 2013 of sabotaging the national defense and damaging federal property; sentenced February 2014 to just over five years in federal prison. The sabotage conviction was vacated on appeal in May 2015 and he was resentenced to time served.

cases.charges: Sabotage of the national defense; damaging federal property (Y-12 Transform Now Plowshares action)
cases.convicted: Yes — May 2013 (sabotage conviction vacated on appeal May 2015)

name: Warren Ballentine
description: Member of the Virgin Islands Five, a group of Black liberation activists convicted of murdering eight people in the September 6, 1972 Fountain Valley massacre at a golf course on St. Croix in the U.S. Virgin Islands. The killings took place during a turbulent period of Black liberation and anti-colonial rebellion in the U.S.-occupied Virgin Islands. The defendants' convictions were obtained after they alleged they had been tortured into confessing. Held in Golden Grove Prison on St. Croix continuously since 1973, more than four decades.

cases.charges: Murder (Fountain Valley massacre, September 6, 1972)

name: Beaumont Gereau
description: Member of the Virgin Islands Five, convicted along with four co-defendants of murdering eight people in the September 6, 1972 Fountain Valley massacre at a golf course on St. Croix in the U.S. Virgin Islands. The killings occurred during a period of Black liberation and anti-colonial agitation in the U.S.-occupied Virgin Islands; defendants alleged they were tortured into confessing. Held in Golden Grove Prison on St. Croix continuously since 1973.

cases.charges: Murder (Fountain Valley massacre, September 6, 1972)
